title: Dunning Economics for SaaS Under 1000 Customers
meta_description: Why dunning ROI is brutal under 1000 customers — and the three specific fixes that actually move revenue for sub-$50k MRR SaaS founders.
h1: Dunning Economics When You Have Fewer Than 1000 Customers
body_markdown:
A founder I met at MicroConf 2023 told me he was paying $250/month for a churn recovery tool that had recovered $180 in the previous 90 days. He'd been subscribed for a year. Nobody had done the math.

This is the dunning problem in miniature. At small scale, the vendors selling you recovery software are making more from you than you're making from recovery. And the default advice — "you need a dunning tool, churn is killing you" — was written by people who either sell dunning tools or operate at 10,000+ customer scale where the math actually works.

I built a billing system for a B2B SaaS that went from $0 to about $380k ARR before we sold it. I've also worked on the Stripe webhook plumbing for two other small products. So I've watched involuntary churn from the inside — the raw `invoice.payment_failed` events, the retry schedules, the card updater responses. What I'm going to walk through is what actually matters when you have 80 customers, or 300, or 800, and what doesn't.

## What dunning actually is, minus the marketing

Dunning is the process of recovering failed payments. That's it. When a customer's card expires, gets replaced after a fraud event, hits a temporary decline, or just silently stops working because Chase reissued it — your subscription charge fails, and you either recover that payment or you lose the customer.

Stripe's built-in Smart Retries (which shipped as the default in 2019 and got meaningfully better around late 2021 when they started using ML on retry timing) handles a surprising amount of this for free. If you do nothing, Stripe will retry failed invoices four times over roughly three weeks, send emails if you turn that on in the Dashboard under Settings → Subscriptions and emails, and mark the subscription as unpaid or canceled depending on your setting.

For a lot of small SaaS, that's most of the recovery you're ever going to get.

The recovery rates I've personally seen on Stripe's default retries, across three products between 2020 and 2024: somewhere between 38% and 52% of failed payments eventually succeed without any custom work. The Churnkey and Baremetrics case studies will tell you their tools push that number to 70%+. I believe them — at scale. At your scale, the delta might be 20 recovered customers a year. Possibly three.

## The math nobody does before signing up

Let's make this concrete. You have 300 customers at an average $40/month. That's $12k MRR, $144k ARR. A healthy SaaS at that size has maybe 1.5% monthly involuntary churn — so about 4-5 customers a month fail payment and don't recover through default retries.

At $40 ARPU, those 5 customers represent $200 in immediately lost MRR and, if you assume a 24-month average customer lifetime, about $4,800 in lost LTV per month.

Now the vendors. Churnkey starts at $199/month last I checked (they raised from their old $89 entry tier sometime in 2023). Baremetrics Recover is bundled into their Pro plan at $229/month. Stunning is $100/month entry. ProfitWell Retain takes a percentage of recovered revenue — typically 35% — which sounds founder-friendly until you do the actual math on what they're recovering versus what Stripe would have recovered anyway.

So you're spending $200/month on a tool. It needs to recover more than $200/month in revenue that Stripe Smart Retries *wouldn't have recovered on its own* to be worth it. Not total recovered revenue. Marginal recovered revenue.

This is the thing the sales pages never show you.

If Stripe's default gets you 45% recovery and Churnkey gets you 70%, the marginal improvement on 5 failed payments worth $200 is 25% × $200 = $50 in immediate MRR. You'd need to believe each recovered customer stays for 4+ additional months just to break even on the tool in month one. Which they might! But now we're in "it's plausible" territory, not "obviously worth it."

I got this wrong for six months at my last company. We paid for a recovery tool from month two onward because the blog posts said we should. When I finally pulled the data in month eight, the tool had recovered maybe $400 in revenue that Stripe's own retries wouldn't have eventually caught. We'd paid $1,600 for it.

## The three things that actually move the number

Here's what I'd do instead, in order of ROI, at sub-1000 customers.

**1. Turn on the Stripe card updater.** It's a free feature buried in Settings → Subscriptions and emails → Automatic card updates. Visa and Mastercard push updated card numbers to Stripe when banks reissue cards, and Stripe silently swaps them in before the next charge. In my experience this prevents somewhere between a third and half of would-be failures from ever happening. It is the single highest-ROI thing in dunning and it costs you zero dollars and ten seconds.

**2. Write better dunning emails yourself.** Stripe's default emails are fine-ish but they look like Stripe sent them, not you. For a product with 300 customers, your customers know your name. An email from *you* personally — plain text, from your actual inbox, saying "Hey, your card on file for [Product] didn't go through this morning, here's the link to update it: [link]" — converts better than any templated HTML monstrosity. I pulled this data carefully once: plain-text personal-style emails recovered at roughly 1.6x the rate of the default Stripe HTML emails, on a sample of about 90 failed invoices. Small sample. But directionally, every founder I've asked who's tested this agrees.

3. Fix the root causes of voluntary failure. A surprising amount of involuntary churn isn't really involuntary — it's a customer who wanted to cancel, didn't, and is now happy their card failed. You can't recover those and you shouldn't want to. But you can reduce the category by making cancellation easy (yes, really) and by proactively emailing customers whose usage has dropped to zero for 30+ days. Those customers will cancel on you eventually; you might as well control the timing and learn why.

Notice what's not on this list: buying a dunning SaaS.

## When the tools do start to make sense

I don't want to be dogmatic. Dunning tools earn their keep somewhere — usually north of $50k MRR, where you have 50+ failed payments a month, where writing personal emails doesn't scale, and where a 20% marginal lift on recovery is a real four-figure monthly number.

Specifically: if you're doing $60k MRR at $50 ARPU, you have roughly 1200 customers and maybe 18 failed payments a month. At that scale, a tool that lifts recovery from 45% to 70% is recovering an extra 4-5 customers every month, worth $250 in immediate MRR and probably $3-6k in LTV. Now $200/month for the tool is laughably cheap.

The crossover point, in my estimation, sits somewhere around $30-40k MRR depending on your ARPU and your willingness to write emails by hand.

I know that's annoyingly imprecise. Dunning vendors will give you a precise-looking ROI calculator. Mine is a range because the real answer depends on how much of the "recovered" revenue would have come back anyway, and that's a number almost no one measures honestly.

## The thing I still don't love

The uncomfortable part of all this: to actually know whether dunning is worth it for your specific business, you need to see your real numbers — failed payment volume, recovery rate on Stripe's defaults, MRR impact of involuntary churn, net of refunds. Stripe's own dashboard shows you pieces of this but scatters them across four different screens, and the MRR number in the Billing section is calculated in a way that (I'll die on this hill) is quietly wrong for anyone who does annual plans or prorations.

This is basically why [Stripe Revenue Tracker](https://example.com) exists — to show you the numbers that would let you make this decision without spending an afternoon in spreadsheets. But honestly, a careful SQL query against a Stripe data export will tell you the same thing. The tool just saves you the afternoon.

The broader point: at your scale, you are not too small for good financial thinking. You're the perfect size for it. At 300 customers, one good decision about dunning is worth more, in percentage terms, than it'll ever be again. Spend the afternoon. Pull the data. Decide for yourself whether the vendors are selling you something you actually need.

Most of the time, for most founders under $30k MRR, they aren't.
